title: Pedestrian behaviors at and perceptions towards various pedestrian facilities: an examination based on observation and survey data
research_problem: This paper presents findings from an observational study of pedestrian behaviors at various urban crosswalks and a pedestrian user survey which sought pedestrian perceptions toward various pedestrian facilities in a divided urban boulevard located next to a large university campus (Michigan State University, East Lansing, MI, USA).
method_summary: The study used two data sources: (1) video-camera records taken along the study site to analyze pedestrian crossing compliance behavior, and (2) a user survey completed by pedestrians using the study site (711 completed questionnaires from daily and occasional users). The study calculated Spatial Crossing Compliance Rate (SCCR) and Temporal Crossing Compliance Rate (TCCR) based on pedestrian movements in crosswalk influence areas.
tasks: Analyze pedestrian crossing compliance rates at various crosswalk types; Assess pedestrian perceptions toward different pedestrian facilities; Identify factors influencing pedestrian crossing choices; Compare survey responses with observed behavioral data
datasets: Grand River Avenue (M-43) in East Lansing, Michigan - 1km divided boulevard section extending from Abbott to Bogue Streets
metrics: Spatial Crossing Compliance Rate (SCCR) - percent of pedestrians who crossed within the crosswalk area (CA) divided by total pedestrians in the crosswalk influence area (CIA); Temporal Crossing Compliance Rate (TCCR) - ratio of pedestrians in CA who comply with pedestrian WALK signal indication; Pedestrian crossing volumes; User survey responses (frequency, preferences, perceptions)
claim_text: Unsignalized midblock crosswalks were the treatment of preference to pedestrians (83% reported a preference to cross) and showed high crossing compliance rate (71.2%)
limitations: Study population was primarily undergraduate and graduate students, staff and faculty at Michigan State University, which may limit generalizability to other pedestrian populations | The calculation of crossing compliance rates is based on assumptions about crosswalk influence areas, which may not perfectly represent actual pedestrian behavior | The study was conducted in a specific urban context (university campus adjacent area) and findings may not be generalizable to different urban environments or rural areas | Return rate of 17.9% for the electronic survey may introduce non-response bias
notes: The study site included four signalized intersections, two unsignalized T-intersections, four marked unsignalized midblock crosswalks (two with shelters), and two non-striped midblock crosswalks; Physical barriers studied included vegetation and two-foot high concrete wall; Other treatments included midblock crosswalk shelters, colored paving (red brick pavement) at medians and curbs, and pedestrian warning signs at midblock crossing locations; The average spatial crossing compliance rate from movement data was 71.4%; The average temporal crossing compliance rate at signalized intersection crosswalks from movement data was 50.9%; Good agreement found between survey responses and actual observed behavior, supporting validity of survey data

title: Pedestrian behaviors at and perceptions towards various pedestrian facilities: an examination based on observation and survey data
research_problem: This paper presents findings from an observational study of pedestrian behaviors at various urban crosswalks and a pedestrian user survey which sought pedestrian perceptions toward various pedestrian facilities in a divided urban boulevard located next to a large university campus (Michigan State University, East Lansing, MI, USA).
method_summary: The study used two data sources: (1) video-camera records taken along the study site to analyze pedestrian crossing compliance behavior, and (2) a user survey completed by pedestrians using the study site (711 completed questionnaires from daily and occasional users). The study calculated Spatial Crossing Compliance Rate (SCCR) and Temporal Crossing Compliance Rate (TCCR) based on pedestrian movements in crosswalk influence areas.

metrics: Spatial Crossing Compliance Rate (SCCR) - percent of pedestrians who crossed within the crosswalk area (CA) divided by total pedestrians in the crosswalk influence area (CIA); Temporal Crossing Compliance Rate (TCCR) - ratio of pedestrians in CA who comply with pedestrian WALK signal indication; Pedestrian crossing volumes; User survey responses (frequency, preferences, perceptions)
claim_text: Crosswalk location relative to the origin and destination of the pedestrian was the most influential decision factor for pedestrians deciding to cross at a designated location (90% said so)
limitations: Study population was primarily undergraduate and graduate students, staff and faculty at Michigan State University, which may limit generalizability to other pedestrian populations | The calculation of crossing compliance rates is based on assumptions about crosswalk influence areas, which may not perfectly represent actual pedestrian behavior | The study was conducted in a specific urban context (university campus adjacent area) and findings may not be generalizable to different urban environments or rural areas | Return rate of 17.9% for the electronic survey may introduce non-response bias
notes: The study site included four signalized intersections, two unsignalized T-intersections, four marked unsignalized midblock crosswalks (two with shelters), and two non-striped midblock crosswalks; Physical barriers studied included vegetation and two-foot high concrete wall; Other treatments included midblock crosswalk shelters, colored paving (red brick pavement) at medians and curbs, and pedestrian warning signs at midblock crossing locations; The average spatial crossing compliance rate from movement data was 71.4%; The average temporal crossing compliance rate at signalized intersection crosswalks from movement data was 50.9%; Good agreement found between survey responses and actual observed behavior, supporting validity of survey data

research_problem: This paper presents findings from an observational study of pedestrian behaviors at various urban crosswalks and a pedestrian user survey which sought pedestrian perceptions toward various pedestrian facilities in a divided urban boulevard located next to a large university campus (Michigan State University, East Lansing, MI, USA).
method_summary: The study used two data sources: (1) video-camera records taken along the study site to analyze pedestrian crossing compliance behavior, and (2) a user survey completed by pedestrians using the study site (711 completed questionnaires from daily and occasional users). title: Pedestrian behaviors at and perceptions towards various pedestrian facilities: an examination based on observation and survey data
research_problem: This paper presents findings from an observational study of pedestrian behaviors at various urban crosswalks and a pedestrian user survey which sought pedestrian perceptions toward various pedestrian facilities in a divided urban boulevard located next to a large university campus (Michigan State University, East Lansing, MI, USA).
method_summary: The study used two data sources: (1) video-camera records taken along the study site to analyze pedestrian crossing compliance behavior, and (2) a user survey completed by pedestrians using the study site (711 completed questionnaires from daily and occasional users). The study calculated Spatial Crossing Compliance Rate (SCCR) and Temporal Crossing Compliance Rate (TCCR) based on pedestrian movements in crosswalk influence areas.

claim_text: Signalized intersections with crosswalks help channelize pedestrian traffic but prove unable to persuade pedestrians to comply with the signal indication, particularly under low traffic demand conditions
